message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Multiple data sources suggest that COVID-19 is having adverse effects on mental health. But it is vital to understand what is causing this:
                    <jats:italic>worries</jats:italic>
                    over potential adversities due to the pandemic, or the toll of
                    <jats:italic>experiencing</jats:italic>
                    adverse events.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To explore the time-varying longitudinal relationship between (i) worries about adversity, and (ii) experience of adversity, and both anxiety and depression and test the moderating role of socio-economic position.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Longitudinal cohort study</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Community study</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A well-stratified sample of UK adults recruited into the UCL COVID -19 Social Study (a panel study collecting data weekly during the Covid-19 pandemic) via a combination of convenience and targeted recruitment. The sample was weighted to population proportions of gender, age, ethnicity, education and geographical location.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Exposures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Worries or experiences of adversities during the COVID-19 pandemic</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Outcomes</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Anxiety (GAD-7) and depression (PHQ-9)</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Data were analysed from 41,909 UK adults (weighted data: 51% female, aged 18-99) followed up across 6 weeks (178,430 observations). Using fixed effects regression was used to explore within-person variation over time, cumulative number of worries and experience of adversities were both related to higher levels of anxiety and depression. Number of worries were associated more with anxiety than depression, but number of experiences were equally related to anxiety and depression. Individuals of lower socio-economic position were more negatively affected psychologically by adverse experiences.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions & relevance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Measures over the first few weeks of lockdown in the UK appear to have been insufficient at reassuring people given we are still seeing clear associations with poor mental health both for cumulative worries and also for a range of specific worries relating to finance, access to essentials, personal safety and COVID-19. Interventions are required that both seek to prevent adverse events (e.g. redundancies) and that reassure individuals and support adaptive coping strategies.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key points</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Question</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      How do
                      <jats:italic>worries</jats:italic>
                      over potential adversities due to the COVID-19 pandemic, or the toll of
                      <jats:italic>experiencing</jats:italic>
                      adverse events affect mental health?
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Cumulative number of worries and experience of adversities were both related to higher levels of anxiety and depression during COVID-19, especially amongst individuals of lower socio-economic position.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Meaning</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>During a pandemic, interventions are required that both seek to prevent adverse events (e.g. redundancies) and that reassure individuals and support adaptive coping strategies.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: How are adversities during COVID-19 affecting mental health? Differential associations for worries and experiences and implications for policy

reference.unstructured: Nassif-Pires L , Xavier L de L , Masterson T , Nikiforos M , Rios-Avila F. Pandemic of Inequality. Levy Economics Institute; 2020. Accessed April 22, 2020. https://ideas.repec.org/p/lev/levppb/ppb_149.html
Beck AT . Cognitive Therapy of Depression. Guilford Press; 1979.
Allison PD . Fixed Effects Regression Models. Vol 160. SAGE publications; 2009.
Overview of the UK Population: November 2018. Office for National Statistics; 2018. Accessed May 7, 2020. https://www.ons.gov.uk/releases/overviewoftheukpopulationnovember2018
